title: Fourteen IRGC members killed in unexploded ordnance explosion near Zanjan, Iran
summary: Fourteen Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps members died in an explosion of unexploded ordnance from earlier US-Israeli strikes near the northwestern city of Zanjan, Iran, marking the largest IRGC casualty event since the ceasefire began.
contents:
## Major IRGC Casualty Event From Unexploded Ordnance

### Incident Details
Fourteen Revolutionary Guard members died on May 2 during operations to defuse unexploded ordnance near the northwestern Iranian city of Zanjan, according to state news agency IRNA and the Nournews website. The explosion involved cluster bombs and air mines dropped during the US-Israeli military campaign that began February 28.

### Significance
This incident represents the largest single-day IRGC casualty event since the ceasefire took effect on April 7. The unexploded ordnance originated from strikes conducted during the initial phases of the conflict, highlighting the continuing danger posed by munitions scattered across Iranian territory.

### Broader Impact
Since the war began on February 28, at least 3,375 people have died in Iran according to authorities. An additional 2,600 deaths occurred in Lebanon where fighting between Israel and Hezbollah erupted two days after the initial strikes. Twenty-four people died in Israel, more than 20 in Gulf Arab states, 17 Israeli soldiers in Lebanon, and 13 US service members throughout the region.

### Ordnance Clearance Challenge
The incident underscores the massive unexploded ordnance clearance operation confronting Iran, where US and Israeli strikes targeted military and dual-use infrastructure across multiple provinces over several weeks of active combat.

**Source:** AP News, IRNA, Nournews
